g.data-narration: Welcome to Process Synchronization. Today we'll explore how operating systems manage concurrent processes sharing resources.
Process synchronization manages execution of multiple processes sharing common resources like memory, files, and printers in modern computing environments.
g.text: Managing execution of multiple processes that
Ensures controlled, orderly access to prevent conflicts.

g.data-narration: Without synchronization, race conditions cause data inconsistency. Multiple processes accessing shared data concurrently can produce unpredictable results.
The critical section is code where processes access shared resources. Only one process should execute in its critical section at any time.
g.text: Portion of program where process accesses shared resources/data.
When one process executes in critical section,
no other process should be allowed in theirs.

g.data-narration: Any critical section solution must satisfy: mutual exclusion (no two processes in critical section), progress (only those wanting entry decide), and bounded waiting (limited wait times).
Race conditions occur when outcome depends on timing. In this example, two processes read value 5, increment to 6, and write back, losing one increment.
g.text: Two processes try to increment it:

g.data-narration: TestAndSet is an atomic hardware instruction that atomically sets a lock and returns its old value, enabling mutual exclusion without software busy-waiting.
g.text: old = lock; lock = true; return old;

g.data-narration: Semaphores are synchronization primitives with atomic wait() and signal() operations, introduced by Dijkstra in 1965. They come in binary and counting types.
Wait operation decrements semaphore, blocks if negative. Signal increments and wakes blocked process. These atomic operations prevent race conditions.
The producer-consumer problem uses three semaphores: mutex for mutual exclusion, empty for empty slots, and full for filled slots in the buffer.
Peterson's algorithm is a software solution for two-process mutual exclusion using shared flag[] array and turn variable. Satisfies all three requirements.
The reader-writer problem allows multiple readers but requires exclusive access for writers. First reader locks resource, last reader releases it.
Monitors are high-level synchronization constructs that encapsulate shared data with procedures, providing automatic mutual exclusion. Only one process active inside.
Deadlock is circular wait where processes wait indefinitely for resources held by each other. Starvation is being denied resources despite eligibility.
In summary: Process synchronization ensures correct concurrent execution. Critical section requires mutual exclusion, progress, and bounded waiting. Semaphores and monitors provide solutions.
g.text: Process Sync → Critical Section → Semaphores/Monitors → Classical Problems
